title_ar: باب ما جاء في الكهان ونحوهم
content_en:
The Islamic ruling on fortune-telling, divination, and those who claim knowledge of the unseen is one of the sharpest expressions of the Tawhid principle as it relates to knowledge. Allah alone possesses comprehensive knowledge of the unseen (al-ghayb), and any claim to independent access to that knowledge — whether through astrology, geomancy, palmistry, crystal gazing, or similar methods — is a direct challenge to this divine exclusivity and thus a form of shirk.

The central Quranic declaration on this subject is clear: 'Say: None in the heavens and the earth knows the ghayb except Allah' (Quran 27:65). This absolute statement admits of no exception in principle: the future, what lies beyond human perception, what is hidden from the senses — all of this belongs to Allah's exclusive knowledge. Even the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was told explicitly: 'I do not tell you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor that I know the ghayb' (Quran 6:50). If the Seal of the Prophets ﷺ did not claim comprehensive knowledge of the unseen, then any human being who does so — or any system that purports to reveal it — is trafficking in falsehood.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab presents a range of hadiths that clarify the Islamic position on those who visit fortune-tellers and diviners. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Whoever goes to a fortune-teller and believes what he says has disbelieved in what was revealed to Muhammad' (Abu Dawud). Another narration states: 'Whoever goes to a diviner or fortune-teller and believes what he says, his prayer will not be accepted for forty days.' (Muslim.) These hadiths reveal a graduated scale of prohibition — the act of visiting a fortune-teller is itself forbidden; to go and believe their claims is a more severe violation; and for some scholars, to go with full belief constitutes an act of disbelief.

The chapter distinguishes between the kahin (soothsayer), who claims direct contact with jinn and demons who convey unseen information, and the 'arraf, a general term for one who claims knowledge of hidden matters through various methods. Both are condemned in the authentic Sunnah. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Whoever goes to an 'arraf and asks him about something — his prayer will not be accepted for forty days.' (Muslim.) The scholars note that this ruling applies even if the person does not believe the fortune-teller's claims, because the act of going to them lends legitimacy to the claim and creates social and theological confusion.

An important dimension of this chapter is the explanation of how diviners sometimes appear to 'know' things that seem verified by events. The Prophet ﷺ explained this phenomenon in a famous hadith: the jinn (who can move quickly through the heavens and eavesdrop on the lowest heaven) sometimes catch fragments of true information from the angels, add a hundred lies to the one true thing, and convey it to their human associates — the soothsayers and fortune-tellers — who then present it as mystical insight. The rare truth in their words is enough to deceive the gullible into crediting the many falsehoods. This mechanism, described in multiple sahih hadiths, explains why fortune-tellers occasionally say things that appear accurate.

From the Tawhid perspective, the harm of fortune-telling extends beyond theological error to practical spiritual ruin: it replaces trust in Allah (tawakkul) with dependence on other sources; it introduces anxiety and fatalism based on false 'knowledge'; and it opens the door to deeper involvement with the occult. The chapter thus serves as a practical warning against a category of practices that, however culturally normalized in many societies, fundamentally undermines the Islamic worldview.
